name: NIH Funding Wuhan
description: The theory of NIH Funding Wuhan centers on the claim that the National Institutes of Health (NIH), primarily through grants funneled to EcoHealth Alliance, indirectly funded gain-of-function research at the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) in China, potentially contributing to the emergence of SARS-CoV-2. Proponents argue that these grants, particularly those awarded to EcoHealth Alliance under the leadership of Peter Daszak, were used to conduct risky research involving bat coronaviruses at the WIV, which they believe could have led to a lab leak. They point to sub-awards from EcoHealth Alliance to the WIV and research proposals outlining viral manipulation as evidence. Mainstream scientific consensus acknowledges NIH funding to EcoHealth Alliance, which in turn sub-awarded funds to WIV for bat coronavirus research. However, the central point of contention is whether this research constituted 'gain-of-function' research, specifically research that enhances the transmissibility or virulence of a pathogen. The NIH maintains that the research funded did not meet the definition of gain-of-function requiring prior review, or if it did, it was reviewed and approved. Many scientists argue that the research in question aimed to understand the natural evolution and potential threats of coronaviruses, which is crucial for pandemic preparedness. Critics of the theory emphasize the lack of definitive evidence supporting a lab leak origin of COVID-19, and the strong evidence pointing to natural zoonotic transmission. They also argue that the definition of gain-of-function is often misconstrued and that the research in question was essential for understanding and preventing future pandemics. Investigations by government agencies and independent scientists have yielded conflicting conclusions, with some finding evidence of inadequate oversight and potential risks, while others have found no evidence of deliberate gain-of-function research that led to the pandemic.
subtitle: Controversial funding and pandemic origins.

claims.claim: NIH funding was used for gain-of-function research at WIV.
claims.evidence: Sub-awards from EcoHealth Alliance to WIV for bat coronavirus research; Research proposals outlining viral manipulation.
claims.criticism: NIH maintains research did not meet the definition requiring review; Many scientists argue the research aimed to understand natural evolution of viruses, not enhance virulence/transmissibility; Conflicting definitions of 'gain-of-function' complicate the issue.

claims.claim: The research at WIV led to a lab leak origin of COVID-19.
claims.evidence: Proximity of WIV to the initial outbreak; Concerns about lab safety protocols at WIV; Circumstantial evidence regarding early cases and genetic analysis.
claims.criticism: Strong evidence points to natural zoonotic transmission; Lack of direct evidence linking the research to a lab leak; Scientific consensus favors a natural origin but acknowledges lab leak possibility remains open.

claims.claim: EcoHealth Alliance and Peter Daszak were negligent in their oversight of the research.
claims.evidence: Concerns about transparency and reporting of research findings; Allegations of conflicts of interest; Criticisms of EcoHealth Alliance's relationship with WIV.
claims.criticism: EcoHealth Alliance maintains it followed all regulations and reporting requirements; Investigations have yielded mixed findings regarding oversight; Scientific community debates the extent of responsibility and potential conflicts of interest.

keyFigures.name: Peter Daszak
keyFigures.role: President of EcoHealth Alliance, principal investigator of NIH grants.

keyFigures.name: Anthony Fauci
keyFigures.role: Director of NIAID, oversaw NIH funding decisions.

keyFigures.name: Alina Chan
keyFigures.role: Molecular biologist, proponent of the lab leak theory.

keyFigures.name: Kristian G. Andersen
keyFigures.role: Virologist, co-author of 'The Proximal Origin of SARS-CoV-2', initially dismissed lab leak theory.

timeline.event: NIH pauses funding for gain-of-function research on influenza, MERS, and SARS viruses.
NIH grants awarded to EcoHealth Alliance, with sub-awards to WIV for bat coronavirus research.
Outbreak of COVID-19 in Wuhan, China.
Increased scrutiny of NIH funding and WIV research; Emergence of the NIH Funding Wuhan theory.

sources.author: United States Government Accountability Office
sources.work: COVID-19: Federal Efforts to Assess and Mitigate Risks Associated with Gain-of-Function Research
sources.title: United States Government Accountability Office

sources.title: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability
